---
title: "CoffeeKing: Yelp Coffee-Shop Analytics at Scale"
description: "A Spark/Databricks pipeline over ~442K Yelp reviews that turns raw review text into location, hours, and service recommendations for a coffee startup — ending in a stakeholder deck of prioritized actions."
image: featured.png   # the Q2 national map regenerates and saves itself here on render
date: 2025-07-30
categories: [Data Engineering, Spark SQL, Python, NLP]
# Ship the standalone HTML files with the site (Quarto won't always copy files
# referenced only inside an <iframe>, so list them explicitly).
resources:
  - notebook.html
  - metro-philly.html
---

## The brief

CoffeeKing, a venture-backed coffee startup, needed data-driven guidance on three
launch decisions: **where to open, when to open, and what makes a visit
five-star.** The raw material was Yelp's Open Dataset — multi-gigabyte JSON files
of businesses, reviews, and users — and the task was to turn that firehose into a
short list of actions leadership could actually run with.

This is the project where I work at **industry scale with a modern stack**: the
coffee domain is just the substrate; the transferable story is the engineering and
the translation from messy data to prioritized recommendations.

## Data & pipeline

A deliberate **two-stage pipeline** kept the heavy lifting off the cluster:

1. **Stage 1 (Google Colab):** stream-load the multi-GB `business.json`,
   `review.json`, and `user.json`; filter to the *Coffee & Tea* category; explode
   the nested business-hours structure; and write three Parquet shards.
2. **Stage 2 (Databricks):** load the shards into the lakehouse as Delta tables and
   run the analysis in **Spark SQL and Python**.

The working tables:

| Table | Rows | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `coffee_reviews` | ~442K | Sentiment & demand signal |
| `coffee_shops` | ~6.7K | Supply & quality landscape |
| `coffee_users` | ~259K | Reviewer engagement |

The unglamorous engineering was the real work: parsing the inconsistent
business-hours JSON, deriving ZIP-level rollups, building reusable temp views, and
handling genuine data-quality landmines — including ~1,300 shops whose hours were
placeholder ("Closed all week") or missing, which I flagged rather than silently
averaging over.

## Three questions, answered with SQL + NLP

**Q1 — Operating hours.** Exploding and standardizing per-day hours for shops
rated ≥4.5★ showed top performers cluster in **7 AM–5 PM** windows, peaking on
weekend mornings — the profitable demand window CoffeeKing should staff for.

![The ten most common operating-hour ranges among 4.5★+ shops; the 7 AM–5 PM window leads.](hours-chart.png){fig-alt="Horizontal bar chart of the ten most frequent operating-hour ranges among shops rated 4.5 stars or higher, with 7 AM to 5 PM the most frequent."}

**Q2 — Underserved markets.** A ZIP-level cut for **≥20 shops but ≤3.5★ average**
surfaced high-density, low-satisfaction pockets — the "lots of competition,
mediocre execution" markets a newcomer can disrupt. Twelve ZIPs qualify, and they
cluster: **Tampa leads the country** (33607, 33612, 33511, 33578), with
**Philadelphia** (19102, 19406, 18901), **Nashville** (37214, 37211), and single
standouts in **Tucson (85719)**, **Reno (89503)**, and the **Indianapolis suburbs
(46038)**.

The map below regenerates live from the Spark summary (no raw data needed): color is
the average rating, on the same scale as the metro view; size is review volume.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the R that builds this map"}
library(ggplot2)
library(maps)
library(ggrepel)

# Clinical-greenhouse palette + the metro map's rating ramp
pine <- "#15433C"; ink <- "#15201D"; mint <- "#EDF7F2"; paper <- "#FCFDFB"
rating_ramp <- c("#C44536", "#E9C46A", "#1F7A63")   # red -> amber -> viridian (matches metro)

# Underserved ZIPs with 20+ real shops, straight from the corrected Spark summary
underserved <- tibble::tribble(
  ~zip,    ~shops, ~avg_rating, ~reviews, ~lat,      ~lon,
  "33607", 38, 3.29, 1066, 27.96699,  -82.51889,
  "85719", 37, 3.28, 3169, 32.24636, -110.95075,
  "19102", 29, 3.31, 2548, 39.95128,  -75.16514,
  "33612", 28, 3.25, 2036, 28.05568,  -82.44260,
  "37214", 26, 3.19, 1670, 36.17239,  -86.67947,
  "33511", 25, 3.34,  921, 27.92988,  -82.30437,
  "19406", 23, 3.17,  974, 40.08843,  -75.38783,
  "89503", 23, 3.50, 3461, 39.53170, -119.83161,
  "37211", 23, 3.33, 1447, 36.07439,  -86.72863,
  "18901", 22, 3.32,  687, 40.30908,  -75.13646,
  "33578", 22, 3.39, 1067, 27.87039,  -82.33383,
  "46038", 20, 3.48, 1087, 39.95789,  -86.02128
)

us <- map_data("state")

p_underserved <- ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = us, aes(long, lat, group = group),
               fill = mint, color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(data = underserved,
             aes(lon, lat, size = reviews, color = avg_rating),
             alpha = 0.75, stroke = 0) +
  geom_text_repel(data = underserved, aes(lon, lat, label = zip),
                  family = "JetBrains Mono", size = 3, color = ink,
                  min.segment.length = 0, box.padding = 0.5, seed = 1) +
  scale_color_gradientn(colours = rating_ramp, limits = c(1, 5), breaks = 1:5,
                        name = "Average rating (stars)") +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 10, name = "Reviews") +
  coord_map("albers", lat0 = 29.5, lat1 = 45.5,
            xlim = c(-125, -66), ylim = c(24, 50)) +
  labs(title = "Coffee deserts: saturated ZIPs, soft ratings",
       subtitle = "Twelve U.S. ZIP codes with 20+ coffee shops yet an average rating \u2264 3.5\u2605") +
  theme_void(base_family = "Public Sans") +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = paper, color = NA),
    plot.title      = element_text(family = "Space Grotesk", face = "bold",
                                   color = pine, size = 15),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(color = ink, size = 10, margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.margin     = margin(10, 12, 10, 12)
  )

p_underserved
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![The twelve U.S. ZIP codes with 20+ coffee shops yet an average rating at or below 3.5★. Color marks the average rating (same scale as the metro map below); size marks total review volume. Tampa accounts for four of the twelve.](index_files/figure-html/fig-underserved-1.png){#fig-underserved fig-alt='Albers-projected map of the contiguous United States with twelve labeled points marking saturated, low-rated ZIP codes. Four cluster around Tampa, three around Philadelphia, two around Nashville, with single points near Tucson, Reno, and Indianapolis. All points sit in the amber band of a red-to-green rating scale.' width=768}
:::
:::




Zooming into Philadelphia — one of the clusters, shown as a representative case
rather than the worst (that's Tampa) — exposes the texture the ZIP averages hide:
shop by shop, each point colored by its own rating and sized by review volume.

![Philadelphia's coffee landscape, shop by shop — colored by rating, sized by review volume, on the same scale as the national map.](metro-philly.png){fig-alt="Street-level map of the Philadelphia metro area. Each coffee shop is a point colored from red (low rating) to green (high rating) and sized by review count, densest in Center City and South Philadelphia."}

<details>
<summary>↔ Explore the interactive metro map</summary>

<iframe src="metro-philly.html" width="100%" height="540"
        style="border:1px solid #d8e3dd; border-radius:8px; margin-top:0.75rem;"
        loading="lazy" title="Interactive Philadelphia coffee-shop map"></iframe>

</details>

**Q3 — Sentiment drivers.** Tokenizing a balanced sample of 1★ and 5★ reviews
(NLTK stopword filtering, term-frequency and TF-IDF, word clouds) produced a sharp
contrast: five-star language is about **product and people** ("delicious,"
"friendly," "staff"), while one-star language is about **process failures**
("order," "minutes," "never," "asked") — i.e., service speed and errors, not the
coffee itself.

<!-- Current image is the raw-frequency version. Swap for the log-odds version once
     regenerated in the notebook so the figure matches the "sharp contrast" claim. -->
![Five-star (left) versus one-star (right) review vocabulary.](sentiment-wordclouds.png){fig-alt="Two word clouds side by side. The five-star cloud emphasizes product and friendliness words; the one-star cloud emphasizes service-and-process words like order, waited, minutes, and rude."}

A correlation study added a useful null result: ZIP-level shop density barely
relates to average rating (r ≈ 0.19), so saturation alone doesn't predict quality —
execution does.

## From analysis to recommendations

The project ended where stakeholder work should — with **prioritized actions**, not
just charts:

1. **Fix the data first** — audit and correct the placeholder/missing operating
   hours before making consumer-facing decisions on them.
2. **Staff to the demand curve** — align hours and promotions to the 7 AM–5 PM peak,
   with weekend-morning emphasis.
3. **Enter underserved ZIPs** — target the high-density, low-rating pockets the
   analysis surfaced.
4. **Compete on service speed** — the sentiment analysis says slow/incorrect service
   is the dominant driver of one-star reviews, so it's the highest-leverage
   operational fix.

## Why it matters

This is end-to-end analytics at scale: raw multi-GB JSON → a Spark pipeline →
SQL and NLP analysis → a deck of ranked recommendations a non-technical leadership
team can act on. The translation step — turning 442K rows into four decisions —
is the part most analyses skip and most hiring managers actually want to see.

## Limitations

Stated honestly: Yelp data is self-selected and geographically skewed toward large
metros, so it's a proxy for demand rather than a census; sentiment was measured
with frequency/TF-IDF methods rather than a trained model; and the operating-hours
findings are constrained by the data-quality gaps noted above.
